quiz_questions.question_text: Informed consent requires that participants:

options.text: Know nothing about the study before agreeing
Only give consent after the study is complete
Understand the study's purpose, risks, and procedures before agreeing to participate
Are forced to participate by the researcher

quiz_questions.explanation: Informed consent ensures that participants voluntarily agree after receiving full disclosure about the study's nature, risks, and procedures.

quiz_questions.question_text: An IRB (Institutional Review Board):

options.text: Selects the sample for the study
Publishes the research results
Reviews and approves research to ensure ethical standards are met
Conducts the research itself

quiz_questions.explanation: IRBs protect human subjects by reviewing study designs before data collection begins to ensure ethical treatment of participants.

quiz_questions.question_text: The Tuskegee Syphilis Study is an example of:

options.text: A severe ethical violation that led to modern research guidelines
A study with excellent follow-up procedures
Ethical research with proper informed consent
A well-designed randomized controlled trial

quiz_questions.explanation: The Tuskegee study withheld effective treatment from African American men with syphilis for decades without informed consent, leading to the Belmont Report and modern ethical standards.

quiz_questions.question_text: Data fabrication is:

options.text: The same thing as rounding numbers
Acceptable when deadlines are tight
A serious ethical violation involving inventing data that was never collected
Common and accepted practice in research

quiz_questions.explanation: Fabricating data is scientific fraud that undermines the integrity of research and can have serious real-world consequences.

quiz_questions.question_text: Participants can withdraw from a study:

options.text: Never, once they have started
At any time without penalty
Only with the researcher's permission
Only before the study begins

quiz_questions.explanation: Voluntary participation includes the absolute right to withdraw at any time without facing any penalty or loss of benefits.

quiz_questions.question_text: Selective reporting (cherry-picking data) is:

options.text: An ethical violation that misrepresents findings
Required for peer-reviewed publications
The same as thorough data analysis
Good scientific practice

quiz_questions.explanation: Reporting only favorable results while hiding unfavorable ones distorts the true findings and misleads other researchers and the public.

quiz_questions.question_text: Deception in research:

options.text: May be used in limited cases with IRB approval and must be followed by debriefing
Is always acceptable in any study
Requires no ethical review whatsoever
Is never used under any circumstances

quiz_questions.explanation: Deception must be justified as necessary, approved by an IRB, involve minimal risk, and always be followed by thorough debriefing of participants.

quiz_questions.question_text: The Belmont Report established:

options.text: Statistical formulas for hypothesis testing
Ethical principles for research with human subjects: respect, beneficence, and justice
Standards for manufacturing quality control charts
Methods for stratified random sampling

quiz_questions.explanation: The Belmont Report (1979) laid out three fundamental ethical principles: respect for persons, beneficence (minimizing harm), and justice (fair distribution of research burdens and benefits).

quiz_questions.question_text: P-hacking (trying many analyses until finding significance) is:

options.text: Required by most IRBs
Always acceptable if the sample is large
A thorough approach to data analysis
An ethical and methodological problem that inflates false positives

quiz_questions.explanation: P-hacking exploits random noise by testing many hypotheses until one produces a significant result, dramatically increasing the false positive rate.

quiz_questions.question_text: Ethical research ultimately:

options.text: Protects participants and enhances the credibility of science
Is unnecessary in modern times
Only applies to medical research
Slows scientific progress unnecessarily

quiz_questions.explanation: Ethical standards protect research participants from harm and maintain public trust in the scientific process.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'confidentiality' mean in research?

options.text: Protecting participants' identities and personal information from unauthorized disclosure
Hiding the research results from the public
Sharing data publicly with participants' full names
Not collecting any data at all

quiz_questions.explanation: Confidentiality means that researchers safeguard participants' personal information and do not reveal individual identities in publications or reports.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'anonymizing data' mean?

options.text: Collecting and storing participants' names with their data
Publishing all personal details for transparency
Using only pseudonyms without removing other identifying details
Removing all identifying information so participants cannot be traced back to their responses

quiz_questions.explanation: Anonymization removes names, addresses, and all other identifying information so that individual participants cannot be connected to their data.

quiz_questions.question_text: What are 'vulnerable populations' in research ethics?

options.text: Only healthy adult volunteers who meet strict eligibility criteria for the specific study protocol
No specific group requires extra protection because all participants have equal rights under federal research law
Groups with limited autonomy (such as children, prisoners, and cognitively impaired individuals) who need extra protections
Only researchers who conduct studies and therefore face professional risks from negative research outcomes

quiz_questions.explanation: Vulnerable populations include groups that may have reduced ability to provide fully voluntary informed consent, requiring additional safeguards in research.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'beneficence' mean as a research ethics principle?

options.text: Researchers should maximize their own benefits from the study
All research must generate financial profit
Participants should pay for the cost of research
Researchers should minimize harm and maximize potential benefits to participants

quiz_questions.explanation: Beneficence requires researchers to protect participants' well-being by minimizing risks and maximizing the potential benefits of the research.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'justice' mean in the Belmont Report?

options.text: Minorities should be excluded from research for their protection
Placebos must always be used in clinical trials
The benefits and burdens of research are distributed fairly across groups
Only wealthy populations should be studied

quiz_questions.explanation: The principle of justice requires that no group bears a disproportionate share of research risks, and that the benefits of research are available to all groups.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'debriefing' mean in research?

options.text: Explaining the true nature and purpose of the study to participants after their participation
Concealing the true purpose of the study permanently
Paying participants additional compensation
Asking participants to leave the study immediately

quiz_questions.explanation: Debriefing occurs after participation and ensures that participants understand the true purpose of the study, especially important when any deception was used.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is a 'conflict of interest' in research?

options.text: A situation where a researcher's personal or financial interests could bias the study results
A disagreement between researchers about methods
A required component of all research proposals
A design that improves objectivity

quiz_questions.explanation: Conflicts of interest arise when researchers have financial ties, personal relationships, or other interests that could consciously or unconsciously bias their research.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'reproducibility' mean in science?

options.text: Sharing methods, data, and procedures so others can independently verify results
Destroying raw data after analysis is complete
Keeping all methods secret from other researchers
Never publishing research findings

quiz_questions.explanation: Reproducibility requires transparent reporting of methods and data so that other researchers can repeat the study and verify the findings independently.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'data falsification' mean?

options.text: Reporting all results, including negative findings
Rounding numbers to appropriate decimal places
Collecting data using proper scientific methods
Manipulating or altering actual research data to produce desired results

quiz_questions.explanation: Data falsification involves changing or omitting data points to make results appear more favorable or significant, which is a form of scientific misconduct.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'respect for persons' mean in the Belmont Report?

options.text: Researchers should always agree with participants' opinions
All participants must be treated identically regardless of circumstances
Researchers should be respected by their institutions
Individuals should be treated as autonomous agents and those with diminished autonomy deserve extra protection

quiz_questions.explanation: Respect for persons means acknowledging individuals' right to make their own informed decisions about participation, and providing additional protections for those with diminished autonomy.

quiz_questions.question_text: A pharmaceutical company funds a study on its own drug and finds it is 30% more effective than competitors. However, the lead researcher owns $500,000 in company stock. An independent replication finds only a 5% difference. What ethical issue does this scenario illustrate?

options.text: There is no ethical concern as long as the study was published
A conflict of interest where the researcher's financial stake may have biased the original results
The independent study must be wrong because it is smaller
The company properly disclosed the conflict

quiz_questions.explanation: The researcher's significant financial interest in the company creates a conflict of interest that may have consciously or unconsciously influenced the study design, analysis, or reporting of results.

quiz_questions.question_text: A university psychology department wants to study how people respond to stress. They plan to tell participants they are taking a simple memory test, but will actually subject them to unexpected loud noises and time pressure to induce stress. Participants will be debriefed afterward. What ethical requirement must be met before this study can proceed?

options.text: No requirements, since participants will eventually be debriefed
Only the department chair needs to approve
The study can proceed as long as participants sign a general consent form
IRB approval is required because the study uses deception and could cause psychological distress

quiz_questions.explanation: Studies involving deception and potential psychological stress require IRB review to ensure the deception is justified, risks are minimized, and proper debriefing procedures are in place.

quiz_questions.question_text: A health researcher collects blood samples from 500 patients for a diabetes study. Five years later, she wants to use the stored samples to study a different genetic condition without contacting the patients for new consent. Is this ethically acceptable?

options.text: No, because using samples for a different purpose than originally consented to violates informed consent principles
Yes, because five years is long enough to reuse any data
Yes, as long as the new study is also about health
Yes, because she already has the samples

quiz_questions.explanation: Informed consent is specific to the study described to participants. Using samples for a different purpose without obtaining new consent or having a broad consent provision violates the principle of respect for persons.

quiz_questions.question_text: A researcher studying income inequality collects survey data including names, addresses, incomes, and social security numbers. The data is stored on an unencrypted laptop that is later stolen. Which ethical principle was most directly violated?

options.text: Informed consent only
Confidentiality and data security obligations
The principle of beneficence
Justice

quiz_questions.explanation: Researchers have an ethical obligation to protect participants' personal information through proper data security measures. Storing sensitive data unencrypted is a serious breach of confidentiality.

quiz_questions.question_text: A social media company uses data from its 2 billion users to conduct behavioral experiments without users' knowledge, changing what content they see to measure effects on mood. Users agreed to a general terms of service but were not specifically informed about the experiments. Is this ethically problematic?

options.text: Yes, because specific informed consent for experimentation was not obtained, and the experiment could cause psychological harm
No, because users agreed to the terms of service
No, because companies are exempt from research ethics
Yes, but only if the company is publicly traded

quiz_questions.explanation: General terms of service do not constitute informed consent for specific experiments, especially those that may affect participants' emotional well-being. This mirrors concerns raised by Facebook's 2014 emotional contagion study.

quiz_questions.question_text: A graduate student is running behind on her dissertation deadline. She has collected data from 180 of her planned 200 participants. Rather than recruit more participants, she fabricates data for 20 fictional participants with results similar to her existing data. What ethical violations has she committed?

options.text: Data fabrication, which is scientific fraud regardless of whether the fake data matches real patterns
None, because the fabricated data is similar to the real data
Only a minor procedural violation
An ethical violation only if she gets caught

quiz_questions.explanation: Fabricating data is scientific fraud regardless of the reason or how plausible the fake data appears. It undermines the integrity of the entire research enterprise and can lead to retraction, loss of credentials, and legal consequences.

quiz_questions.question_text: A clinical trial for a new cancer drug shows the drug is significantly extending lives compared to the placebo group. The trial is scheduled to run for two more years. An independent data safety monitoring board recommends stopping the trial early. Why would they make this recommendation?

options.text: To prevent the drug from being approved by regulatory agencies before all required phases are completed
To save money on the trial since the remaining two years of funding could be redirected to other research
Because two more years is too long for any study and diminishing returns make extended data collection unnecessary
Because continuing to give placebos when the treatment is clearly effective would be unethical (denying effective treatment to the control group)

quiz_questions.explanation: The principle of beneficence requires that once a treatment is clearly shown to be effective, it would be unethical to continue denying it to the control group. Data safety boards can recommend early termination.

quiz_questions.question_text: A researcher conducts 20 different statistical tests on the same dataset and finds one significant result (p = 0.04). She publishes only this one finding without mentioning the other 19 tests. What ethical and statistical problems does this present?

options.text: The only problem is that she should have used a stricter threshold such as p = 0.01 to account for multiple comparisons
This is standard practice in all scientific fields, since researchers routinely publish only their strongest individual findings
No problem, because p = 0.04 is below the standard alpha level of 0.05 and therefore qualifies as statistically significant
P-hacking and selective reporting: with 20 tests, finding one significant result by chance is expected, and hiding the other tests misrepresents the findings

quiz_questions.explanation: With 20 independent tests at alpha = 0.05, there is approximately a 64% chance of at least one false positive. Reporting only the significant result and hiding the failed tests is both p-hacking and selective reporting.

quiz_questions.question_text: A medical study in a developing country tests a new malaria treatment by giving the experimental drug to local villagers and comparing their outcomes to untreated villagers. The same study design would never be approved in the researchers' home country because a standard treatment exists. What ethical principle from the Belmont Report is being violated?

options.text: Reproducibility, because the study design cannot be repeated under identical conditions in a different geographic location
No principle is violated if the local government approves, since national sovereignty overrides international research guidelines
Confidentiality, because villagers in small communities can easily be identified from the published research data
Justice, because the burdens of research are being placed on a vulnerable population that would not benefit from the research design used in wealthier countries

quiz_questions.explanation: The principle of justice requires that research burdens and benefits be distributed fairly. Conducting a study in a developing country that would be considered unethical at home exploits a vulnerable population.

quiz_questions.question_text: A technology company collects location data, browsing history, and health app data from users' phones. They anonymize the data by removing names, then sell it to advertisers. A researcher later demonstrates that combining the 'anonymous' datasets can re-identify 87% of individuals. What does this reveal about anonymization?

options.text: Companies have no obligation to protect user data
Removing names alone may not be sufficient for true anonymization when other data points can be combined to re-identify individuals
Anonymization is always completely effective
87% re-identification is an acceptable rate

quiz_questions.explanation: Simply removing names does not guarantee anonymity. Combining multiple data points (location patterns, browsing habits, health data) can uniquely identify individuals, demonstrating that true anonymization requires more sophisticated techniques.
